data.title: State Street: Elevated Markets Continue To Boost Fees
data.description:
Investors had much to cheer in 2025, as global stock markets recorded another year of impressive gains. Buoyant markets have also improved the fortunes of custody bank State Street (STT), which saw its adjusted net income rise 13% last year to nearly $3 billion.

Covering the shares in November, I did express reservations about this. Valuation was the main one, as State Street's cycle-high profitability had also won it a richer valuation from the market. As you can see below, this has been a nice tailwind for the stock, though it makes the investment case trickier for prospective investors. I was also concerned that State Street had more riding on higher asset levels than its closest peer, Bank of New York Mellon (BK). While the latter has also benefited from strong markets, its growth has also had a broader base.

With fresh financials to digest, now is a good time to review things. I remain cautious, with State Street's valuation now running well beyond its recent historical norms.

Buoyant Fee Revenue

State Street recently released its Q4 2025 results, posting $845 million in adjusted net income on revenue of $3.67 billion. Revenue rose 7.5% year-on-year, while adjusted net income grew 240 basis points ahead of this. State Street's full-year results were also strong, with revenue and net income rising 7.4% and 13.5%, respectively.

Strong stock markets have been a key driver of this performance. State Street generates around 40% of its revenue from asset servicing fees, which include its core custody and administrative services. Much of this revenue is billed directly against assets under custody and/or administrative ('AUC/A'), meaning changes to market levels directly influence this line. State Street reported $53.8 trillion in AUC/A last quarter, with equities accounting for roughly 60% of that figure. Based on start-of-period balances, AUC/A grew 10.5% year-on-year, while servicing fees increased 8%, hinting at ongoing margin pressure in this business.

Of course, State Street isn't just a custody bank. It is also an investment manager, reporting over $5.6 trillion in assets under management ('AUM') last quarter. Many readers will be familiar with the SPDR range of exchange-traded funds, which are included in this business.

Equities account for over 60% of State Street's AUM, so rising markets have also been beneficial here. On top of this, State Street is also seeing positive net flows. The fact that passive funds account for around 90% of its AUM has helped, as these products have been taking share from active solutions for years. As a result, State Street saw management fees rise 15% last quarter to $662 million.

Crucially, fee growth is outpacing State Street's costs. Combined, State Street generated $2.05 billion in servicing and management fees last quarter, up 10.3% year-on-year. Meanwhile, underlying operating expenses rose 6.4%. This high degree of operating leverage is why the company's earnings growth has been so strong recently.

Also, because this growth is coming from capital-light business, it is having a positive impact on State Street's profitability. The company earned a 21.5% return on tangible common equity ('ROTCE') last quarter, which, as you can see above, is well above its ten-year average.

Better Interest Trends

Having been a concern of mine last time, interest revenue was better last quarter. Net interest income rose 7% year-on-year to $802 million, with State Street's net interest margin expanding 14 basis points sequentially to 1.1%. This brings its NIM back in line with its ten-year average.

Like other banks, State Street takes deposits and invests them in interest-earning assets. In contrast to other banks, its earning assets skew heavily to cash and investment securities rather than loans. On the liability side, it has been able to lower funding costs, given a fall in short-term interest rates.

At the same time, it isn't losing as much on its assets. Yields on cash and loans have fallen, but State Street has around $40 billion in held-to-maturity securities. These were only yielding 2.19% last quarter, well below prevailing market rates. The ongoing repricing of this part of its asset base should be a multi-year tailwind, so I expect more NIM gains from here. A steeper yield curve will naturally help, and this may also explain why its shares have done so well recently.

Attractive Capital Returns

Because its profitability is so strong right now, State Street can comfortably fund a high shareholder payout while maintaining ample capital. The company did $635 million in capital returns last quarter, over 90% of its net income. This took its full-year distributions to around $2.1 billion, for a 78% total payout ratio. The buyback component is obviously providing an extra boost to share-level metrics (adjusted EPS growth was around 530 basis points higher than adjusted net income growth last year).

Despite spending heavily on dividends and buybacks, State Street's capital cushion has only increased. It was sitting on an 11.7% Common Equity Tier 1 Ratio at the end of last quarter, up 80 basis points year-on-year, leaving it 120 basis points over the mid-point of management's 10%-11% target range. Alongside its healthy ROTCE, this surplus should allow for attractive capital returns in 2026.

Valuation Concerns

Like its business, State Street's stock has also been a strong performer, returning around 40% over the past year. This partly reflects the growth of its business, but valuation uplift has also contributed. For instance, State Street currently trades for $132.41, which is equal to 2.36 times its tangible book value. That's up from roughly 2.05 a year ago, and it's around 30% above its recent historical average.

Relative to past norms, State Street's trailing price-to-earnings ratio is also on the expensive side. The company earned $10.30 per share in adjusted net income last year, for a multiple of almost 13. That compares to a recent historical average of roughly 11. Moreover, State Street's earnings are currently benefiting from a cyclically strong ROTCE. Using its historical average ROTCE (around 17%), its P/E ratio would rise to nearly 15.

Now, I am not suggesting that we will see a reversion to this average in the near term. However, it is important to appreciate that elevated stock markets are supporting State Street's profitability. In turn, equities are themselves benefiting from historically high valuations, as shown below.

For that reason, I prefer to look at book value-based valuations as a guide for whether State Street is reasonably valued or not. Since this would suggest its shares are trading at a premium valuation, I remain cautious. Accordingly, I continue to rate the shares “Hold.”

data.title: Alibaba Qwen Upgrade: The Market Can No Longer Ignore This AI Catalyst
data.description:
It was a highly anticipated AI launch in the China AI scene. The biggest of China's cloud companies, Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA), is trying to recapture the glory days of its pre-tech crackdown preeminence, while also fending off the significant challenges from PDD Holdings (PDD) and also one of the world's most valuable startups, ByteDance (BDNCE). That competition has spilled over to the AI arena, as China's intensely competitive battleground requires a never-ending pursuit of success, or risk getting wiped out by arch rivals who are always fervently creeping up their shoulders.

Therefore, Alibaba's heavily publicized event in the Chinese media is billed as "a major upgrade to its flagship consumer-facing AI application, Qwen App." That already follows a series of significant launches that have undoubtedly bolstered Alibaba's position as one of the frontrunners in China's AI hierarchy. The market reaction this week has been highly positive so far, with BABA stock already consolidated above the $145 lows, as it surged confidently to retest the early November highs, as the market has lent its weight in concurrence with the significance of the upgrade to Qwen.

While there's no direct comparison to infer a resemblance of Alibaba's ecosystem moat in the U.S., I believe it reminds me somewhat of the Google renaissance (GOOGL, GOOG) that has percolated across Silicon Valley. As a reminder, Google owns the full stack, from custom chips to distribution. And that stranglehold across the ecosystem has afforded the Mountain View company to strengthen its AI prowess, while it closes in on OpenAI's (OPENAI) much vaunted leadership.

In my previous Alibaba write-up, I noted that while the stock's valuation has mean-reverted, I don't think the market has fully revalued BABA's AI narrative. And the recent event goes to show that my conviction isn't without a valid basis, as the efforts are designed to leverage on its ecosystem moat, similar to how Google has astutely made Gemini a force to be reckoned with among its leading AI peers right now.

As for Alibaba, I believe the Hangzhou-headquartered company realized that if there's a moment to finally capitalize on its ecosystem moat to seize the domestic AI narrative now, it couldn't have arrived at a more opportune time. And I believe the focal points in China's overarching AI strategy truly play into Alibaba's underlying strengths, right? China appears to be racing ahead into applied AI.

I believe the world's second-largest economy and unquestionably America's most significant AI rival, is seeking to achieve a decisive breakthrough in embodied AI, ahead of its U.S. peers. The recent CES at Las Vegas was a showcase of AI's ability to integrate its software and hardware prowess, as the demonstrations show their pace in rapidly infusing "artificial intelligence into real-world products." It also aligns with the forecasted 18% CAGR of China's AI hardware market through the rest of the decade. Notably, it could very well cement China's position as the world's preeminent player in the realm of physical AI.

Hence, there's little wonder that Alibaba's latest foray seeks to proliferate AI across its whole ecosystem, that also entails a "deep integration of core services." And the efforts are closely aligned with China's AI strategy of making artificial intelligence practical and user-friendly, potentially pivotal to rapidly diffusing AI across the economy.

Here's the big deal for China AI investors. Because we should no longer view Qwen as a "standalone" or disparate app from Alibaba's business model. Instead, we now have the suite of "Taobao, Taobao Instant Commerce, Alipay, Fliggy, and Amap" being consolidated into what Alibaba believes is a "unified AI interface."

Hence, while China's most cutting-edge AI capabilities appear to still be falling behind its American peers, it has always endeavored to successfully catch up. And the level of AI CapEx is still a fraction of what America's largest AI hyperscalers have committed, and are expected to spend even more through the rest of the decade. Thus, it does seem like Alibaba's RMB380B in CapEx commitment is seemingly much less significant. The U.S.'s ability to fence in China's AI compute capacities with export restrictions on AI chips and advanced lithography systems has created distinct advantages for the U.S. AI leaders. Yet, it still hasn't stymied China's efforts to narrow the gap in model capabilities, while also being capable to quickly deploying these capabilities into the applied AI space.

And if there's one company that could truly unleash applied AI, I believe it has got to be Alibaba. Given its e-commerce clout and tremendous influence and leadership in cloud computing, such an integration will arguably make Qwen an increasingly valuable interface. Its MAUs have also reportedly scaled to the 100M mark, with massive potential to accelerate and expand further. In other words, the best is yet to be, and Alibaba intends to race ahead before its peers could even catch their breath.

Also, Wall Street is increasingly confident that Alibaba Cloud will become an even more prominent growth driver through 2026. And I believe Qwen's integration and bolstered agentic capabilities will be put to the test, as MAUs and monetization opportunities will be closely watched, as these remain in their infancy.

Nevertheless, with Alibaba reportedly owning 40% of the cloud computing market share in China for 2025, these optionalities could see China's e-commerce behemoth garnering "80% of incremental industry revenue growth in 2026," potentially propelling Alibaba's market share to the 60% mark, a crucial milestone that should underpin its valuation re-rating.

Therefore, I'm getting the right price action signals in BABA's print that's shouting out on its uptrend continuation thesis. Observe BABA's distinct past pullbacks, bottoms, and then recovery in a familiar sequence. Such occurrences have duly recurred since April 2024, with buyers taking full advantage of steep pullbacks (including the one that bottomed in December) as opportunities to load up and ride the subsequent recovery.

BABA's forward earnings multiple of 24x is still markedly lower than the Magnificent Seven's 28x, and also Amazon's (AMZN) 32x P/E. Granted, they operate in different markets and also have substantive geopolitical considerations that could render such a direct juxtaposition less relevant than it seems. Yet, the premium over BABA's 10Y average of 20.4x doesn't suggest to me that the market has gone bonkers on its AI growth optionalities. And what does that mean? I think the BABA narrative has a lot more to run, even after the stellar >100% gains that the stock has managed over the past year.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Thales S.A.: Solid Defense And Avionics Growth Solidify Margin Forecast
data.description:
Thales (THLEF) continues to see growth driven by solutions sales as opposed to aftermarket, deferring latent margin bumps from higher aftermarket contribution in the mix as discussed in our previous coverage. Higher-margin solutions such as avionics should continue to drive mix effects and margin, and additionally, the idea to merge their space business with some peers should also make space's contribution more robust, though as a J.V. Destocking trends within payments may continue, but sequential cyber and security improvement is expected. Thales may still have a relative case, but we think there are other ideas that continue to be more industrially levered to the defense spending boom while trading at similar valuations.

Results

The first thing to mention might be the merging of the space businesses with peers, in which Thales will have a 32.5% stake in a J.V.—expected to conclude after next year.

You discussed and I could elaborate just a bit on the synergies. We mentioned mid-triple-digit level of bottom line synergies 5 years after the closing.

Senior EVP of Finance, Pascal Bouchiat

That's quite considerable and amounts to about ~10% of the forecast revenues for the merged businesses, based on pooled R&D benefits and other redundancy eliminations.

Aerospace, overall in Q3, above our expectations in terms of level of growth in this business. By the way, not that much driven by aftermarket...

overall looking at our Aerospace segment end of September, we said 6.9% [in growth of sales]. And I explained that Avionics growth was significantly above this level and Space -- below Space in line with our expectations, meaning that Space end of September is around 2%.

Pascal Bouchiat

The other effect is the pickup in avionics in the mix, which had been driving the margin improvement in the H1. Forecast margin for the FY is expected to be around 100-300 bps higher for the FY, which is higher than the adjusted margin as of H1 at 12.1%.

Okay. So overall just to give you a rule of thumb, but probably a level of EBIT margin for the full year 2025, around 14% is probably what I have in mind. All of that being very much consistent with 12.2%, 12.4% overall at a group level.

Pascal Bouchiat

Solutions and not aftermarket are driving the growth, which could mean a latent effect from aftermarket could bolster margin as aftermarket tends to be higher revenue and usually lags solutions growth once the base of established solutions has grown.

The spending boom in European defense is being driven by laggards in military spending catching up to the more robust spenders in the quite independent France and the UK. Defense outpaced aerospace in terms of order intake growth, but comps will get tough for defense in Q4 due to a record quarter last year, and the order intake is generally lumpy. Orders in space haven't been booked yet related to SpaceRISE and could become booked next quarter or Q1 2026. Drones are also becoming more important in the mix at a triple-digit amount in sales, so maybe around 10% of sales when annualising H1 sales data.

In DIS, things are more troubled and performing below expectations on continued pressure on destocking of cards, not expected to go away imminently. Integration efforts from the Imperva acquisition are also weighing on sales due to sales force integrations. However, margins had been improving on cost and premiumisation initiatives such as those in Australia. The only growth area here is digital identity over the last 9 months.

Bottom Line

The drone exposure, which refers to loitering munitions, is pretty interesting, and aspects of it are going to be recurring due to the disposable nature of these military assets.

What we do is more to provide the equipment, the solutions, but also the system integration. So what does it mean? It means that in terms of specific solutions, we provide, of course, a set of different sensors. That's the first, the type of business we develop in this drone business. Second is what we call C2, command and control. So providing the intelligence to the systems. And third is military capabilities and in particular, through the development and the sales of what we call warhead in particular, to equipped at drones or loitering ammunitions.

Pascal Bouchiat

Other than that, growth has been good in civil and military applications. But there are other solid exposures at similar valuations that also have industrial scaling opportunities in munitions and rockets, such as BAE Systems (BAESY). On the other hand, Thales is cheaper than HENSOLDT (HAGHY). Industrial exposure is desirable given the high degree of certainty around the sales drivers. Thales has some of that in the sensor business to some extent, but munitions and rockets have more repeatable and disposable components and are more a focus in some competitors. We'll leave it lie for the moment, though if the synergies materialise for the underlying business within space, the effective multiple would start getting a discount of 10-15% from BAE.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Clear Secure: Beyond The Airport And The eGate Revolution
data.description:
ClearSecure (YOU) is a US-based digital identity company I have been following for over the past two years. My first coverage of the stock in 2024 rated the stock a buy, citing membership growth from strong travel demand as a key success factor.

Today, YOU is up over 70% since my first coverage, trading at over $33 per share as of late, surpassing my 1-year price target of $22.7 per share then by a significant margin.

Shares performance has been lackluster since going public in 2021, with a price return of merely -58% over that period. A lot of that also has to do with the 30% surge seen over the past year, despite the relatively muted YTD performance. YOU is currently down -1.8% on a YTD basis.

I maintain my buy rating with a 1-year price target of $38 per share, which is a potential upside of 13.6% from today’s level. I believe that YOU should benefit from a few catalysts into 2026, while risks remain minimal to moderate.

Financials Review

Fundamentals are solid overall. In Q3, YOU delivered $229.2 million of revenue, a 15.5% YoY growth. It appears that YOU has seen growth stabilizing from the 30% to 40% growth seen in previous years. Nonetheless, this is still a healthy growth, in my opinion, considering that the driver here is mainly the price increase for CLEAR+ to enhance ARPU.

Meanwhile, profitability appears to have improved. Net margin was 19.7% in Q3 2025, suggesting that YOU has maintained its profitability YoY. The same time last year, YOU delivered a 19% net margin as well. A significant improvement is seen in gross margin, which has currently been over 60% over the past two quarters, with Q3 2025’s 62% being a 5-year-high quarterly gross margin for the company. The biggest driver for this overperformance has been the e-gate automation that requires fewer human operations at the kiosks, reducing the overall cost per-member.

Balance sheet and liquidity remain robust, in my opinion. YOU ended Q3 with over $530 million of cash and short-term investments with zero debts, maintaining a relatively steady level over the past year. While operating cash flow (OCF) was negative for the quarter, it was merely due to the cyclicality around the partnership liability settlements. If we zoom out over a nine-month period ending Q3, YOU actually delivered $174 million of OCF, an over 9% YoY growth.

Catalyst

Overall, I believe that catalysts are relatively moderate to high for YOU in 2026, as there could be a few opportunities to unlock the management’s near-term growth and profitability guidance in FY 2025 and into 2026.

In my view, the first one is the wider deployment of automated eGates in 2026, which financially could help YOU realize operating leverage by reducing the number of human staff per station, as the management alluded to in earnings call:

This quarter, we delivered operating leverage across every line of our P&L, both, again, sequentially and year-over-year. And I think innovations such as eGates really gives us the opportunity to either reallocate capital and/or flow it through to the bottom line.

Source: Q3 earnings call.

As of Q3, YOU has made 10 deployments of these eGates, resulting in an upward trend in gross margins as of late. As such, I believe this will be the key for YOU to drive more expansion into the bottom line to achieve the management’s target of an above-30% adjusted EBITDA for the FY.

The second one, in my opinion, would be the Medicare deal, which will allow YOU to access a different market than the digital travel identity. Eventually, if the rollout proves to be successful in 2026, the narrative around investing in YOU becomes a play in universal digital identity instead of just travel, driving potential re-rating. Financially, the diversification should also help YOU reduce the cyclicality from the exposure to the travel industry.

Risk

Overall, risks are minimal to moderate. While the transition to eGates and the Medicare deal are both interesting growth stories for YOU, I believe there are some chances for setbacks due to implementation delays, which may result in YOU not realizing the savings.

Another area of concern that could be more of a longer-term risk for YOU is the notion of possible competition with TSA despite YOU serving as their partner, in my opinion. In light of the membership price increase last year in July. In general, the marginal benefit of YOU may continue to be tested if the technology of TSA PreCheck Touchless ID, which is the more affordable option, continues to get better over time.

As such, the Medicare deal could be quite critical for YOU, since it will prove to the market that its non-travel identity product is promising enough to offset any potential churn risk from the core business, driving a re-rating potential based on diversification strategy.

Valuation / Pricing

My 1-year target price for YOU is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs. bear scenarios, based on consensus estimate. Accounting for all share classes conversion, I project around 132 million of shares outstanding:

  1. Bull scenario (70% probability) assumptions - YOU successfully expands outside the travel market, with the Medicare rollout seeing a huge success, consequently unlocking similar universal identity contracts with more government institutions. YOU is now valued as a universal identity as opposed to just a travel identity player. I assign YOU a P/S of 6x to reflect the premium rewarded by the market, a bit higher than where it is today at around 5x.

  2. Bear scenario (30% probability) assumptions - YOU’s Medicare implementation faces technical challenges, such as privacy-related issues or hardware malfunction, delaying growth and creating public scrutiny for the app. I assign YOU a forward P/S of 3x, near its 1-year low.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $38 per share, suggesting a 13.6% potential upside from the current price level. I give the stock a buy rating. YOU appears to offer an attractive upside at the price point.

Conclusion

Having secured the Medicare contract, YOU is on the verge of transitioning from a digital travel identity to a more universal identity platform, presenting a re-rating potential. The scale up of eGates further presents an opportunity for a margin expansion into 2026. While implementation delay and competition from TSA’s Touchless ID could be risk factors, I believe that the reward from YOU’s expansion outside the travel identity market should outweigh the risk. I maintain my buy rating for the stock.

data.title: State Street Q4: Expense Growth Improves, But Good News Is Priced In
data.description:
Shares of State Street (STT) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining over 40%. The trust and custody bank has benefited from rising market levels, which mechanistically increase fee revenue, as well as cost efficiencies, which have enhanced margins. While STT beat Q4 estimates, shares traded lower in early trading, reflecting the fact that after their strong run, good news may be in the price. I last covered shares of State Street in October when I rated them a “Hold,” given fee compression and valuation. However, that call was overly timid with STT rallying over 20% since then, justifying a “Buy” rating. With updated financials and a 2026 outlook, now is a good time to revisit State Street.

In the company’s fourth quarter, State Street earned $2.97 per share, which beat estimates by $0.13 as revenue grew over 7% to $3.7 billion. Back in October, I saw the company earning just over $10 this year, and STT ultimately earned $10.30. As has been the trend, STT saw ongoing fee compression. While fee revenue jumped 8% to $2.9 billion, assets under custody & administration rose by 16% to $54 trillion, and assets under management increased 20% to $5.7 trillion. That said, expenses were up just 6% to $2.5 billion, leading to 105bps of operating leverage. While expenses have run hot due to the need to increase tech investments, the majority of this incremental spending has occurred.

Alongside BNY Mellon (BK) and Northern Trust (NTRS), STT is one of the three main trust and custody banks. It primarily holds assets on behalf of clients, and it does not have a retail branch presence. As a result, it is less exposed to credit quality than traditional banks, and its revenue is largely fee-based. This is a low-risk business, and fees are small, making cost efficiency essential to maintaining strong margins.

Fees are rising but lag asset growth

In Q4, servicing fees grew by 8% to $1.4 billion, primarily due to rising market levels. AUC/A was up closer to 20%, speaking to ongoing fee compression. STT does continue to win new business, winning $87 million of ongoing revenue. For the full year, it won $484 billion of assets, which should drive $333 million of revenue. Its current backlog of wins yet to be installed is $320 million, or about 5% of run-rate revenue. The pace of organic growth has been solid, given the maturity of the business.

STT has been adding other services to expand margins, and we have seen solid growth, though it is now moderating. For instance, FX trading fees rose 13% to $405 million. However, it is $23 million below Q2 levels when tariff uncertainty caused tremendous volatility and more trading. That level of trading was unsustainably high, and such volatility is not normal. I expect 2026 revenue to run around $400 million/qtr. Similarly, securities finance revenue was up 8% from last year but down 8% sequentially. While it has been able to grow client lending, spreads face some pressure, given heightened bank interest in securities lending, likely to pressure revenue a bit in 2026.

Aside from its custody banking activities, STT also has an investment management arm with a leading ETF franchise. Fees were up a strong 15% from last year, aided by ongoing market gains as well as larger performance fees, given the strength of gains. Its ETF business added $51 billion of assets, led by ongoing growth in its core, low-cost products that track the S&P (SPY) and gold (GLD). Its institutional business, which has higher fees, also saw $10 billion of inflows. Even with rates declining, demand for cash is strong with $35 billion of fund flows into its money markets.

Interest margins improved while cost growth slowed

Now aside from fee revenue, State Street custodies cash on behalf of clients, which it then earns interest on. STT primarily invests in securities with relatively few loans. Reflecting its relatively small and high-quality loan book, State Street provisioned just $8 million for credit losses. Deposits were up $17 billion from last year to $254 billion with noninterest-bearing balances stable. It has been able to aggressively pass on rate cuts to customers. As such, STT reported a 1.10% net interest margin (“NIM”), up 14bps from last year. Given fixed reinvestment, I expect modest NIM expansion (0-5bps) in 2026, assuming two rate cuts.

As you can see below, expenses were up 6% from last year, led by 11% growth in tech spending due to needs to invest in expanded and updated infrastructure. For the full year, GAAP expenses rose by 12% even with headcount declining by 2%, an unusually wide gap, reflecting this elevated tech spending, as well as more variable spending tied to strong trading activity. Importantly, this spending slowed down in Q4 relative to the full year, as efficiency programs take hold and tech needs are met. I expect this deceleration to persist in 2026.

State Street has a strong balance sheet with a tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio of 11.7%. As a reminder, STT targets a 10%-11% capital ratio. With this capital position, STT can aggressively return capital to investors. Indeed, the company bought back $235 million of stock in Q3, resulting in a 92% payout ratio. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down 4% from last year. Shares also offer a 2.5% dividend yield, which I view as highly secure.

Guidance is credible, but shares are fairly valued

Alongside results, State Street provided guidance for 2026, which largely matched my own expectations. It expects fees to be up 4%-6%, assuming a market that holds flat in 2026 (primarily due to a favorable H1 comparison) with NII up slightly assuming a couple of rate cuts. As anticipated, expenses should rise more slowly in 2026 with its tech needs normalizing. All else equal, each 1% change in the S&P 500 would likely move fee revenue by 0.4%-0.5%, so a substantial market rally or decline would push fee revenue outside of the guided range.

State Street's results in Q4 were consistent with the trend we have been seeing. Higher markets are lifting fee revenue even as fees compress while NIM gradually expands from reinvestment and deposit rate cuts. Finally, 2025 was marked by elevated expenses, but the pace here is starting to slow down, which should support some margin gains in 2026. Meanwhile, its strong balance sheet supports capital returns. I expect these dynamics to persist in 2026, and my views are aligned with guidance, though I expect fee revenue to run towards the lower end, assuming flat markets, given caution on FX revenue.

As a result, I am expecting about $11.50 in 2026 EPS, +/- $0.20 (recognizing the market can shift revenue and EPS). This should support ~$10.50 in capital returns, giving shares a 7.7% capital return yield. I view a 7%-8% capital return yield as appropriate, given underlying revenue growth of about ~3%, assuming moderate market appreciation and ongoing fee compression. This leaves shares inside my fair value range.

My past “Hold” rating was a bit too cautious, given the decelerating expense growth, which is now supporting EPS. However, that positive appears to be factored into shares after their rally. As such, I see the good news reflected. With a 7+% capital return yield, STT remains a suitable “Hold” for investors, but I do not see enough upside to “Buy.” On a pullback below $127, I would be inclined to add shares.

data.title: Sarepta Preliminary Results: Not What The Market Wanted To Hear
data.description:
Thesis

Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc. (SRPT) guided preliminary 4Q25 and FY25 sales a few days ago, and as you know, the figures were a bit below Wall Street’s expectations. It was an all-around softer-than-expected 4Q25 performance. The company estimates that 4Q25 net product revenue will fall in the range of $369.6 million, a figure about $10 million below the consensus of $380.6 million. Elsewhere, FY25 revenue is expected to hit $1.86 billion, which was the big disappointment as the consensus figure was roughly $2.12 billion.

We have to keep in mind also that this disappointing update is coming at a time of heightened safety scrutiny after the FDA added a boxed warning to Elevidys late last year, after we got some reports of fatal acute liver failure in non-ambulatory patients. Other than the drop in earnings, I think the general scrutiny around Elevidys is what is weighing on investor sentiment the most, even though the company reaffirmed to us that they’re still keeping with a $500 million annual sales floor for the therapy in 2026.

Lackluster Preliminary Results

Sarepta has just recently announced a preliminary 4Q25 and FY25 update, and the initial market reaction was bad to say the least, with the stock dropping 15% the day of the press release. However, I felt that the report showed us both the scale and resilience of its commercial franchise, particularly if you look at its Duchenne muscular dystrophy line, which has been hit with pretty bad operational and seasonal headwinds late in the year.

The company reported total net product revenue of about $369.6 million in 4Q25 and hit $1.86 billion for FY25. Now, this figure was actually driven by a near-even split between Elevidys and its PMO exon-skipping portfolio. Elevidys managed to generate $898.7 million for the year, with the last quarter bringing in sales of about $110.4 million. Now, as you probably know, this figure was dampened by a severe flu season and the deferral of six patient infusions into early 2026. That goes to show just how sensitive the therapy is to short-term clinical logistics rather than actual demand.

Elsewhere, the PMO franchise delivered $965.6 million in recurring revenue, which gave them that stabilizing cash flow base. Importantly, however, Sarepta still managed to end 2025 with a hefty cash sum. They now hold approximately $953.8 million in cash and investments and another quarter of positive cash flow, which is a pretty strong balance sheet figure and should fund ongoing commercialisation. Based on that cash sum, Sarepta still seems to have a multi-year cash runway. Even if we assume a relatively high annual net cash burn of $300 million to $400 million, they would still have about 2.5 to 3 years of runway, with a lot more upside if product revenues continue to offset operating expenses.

Management also reaffirmed that the $500 million annual sales floor for Elevidys in 2026; however, they withheld more detailed guidance on this until the impact of new initiatives becomes clearer. I don’t think this helped investor confidence at all. I have been quite bullish on the company in recent times and signaled to me a bit of cautious confidence with everything that's going on in terms of regulatory, safety, and execution considerations.

Elevidys Delayed Sales

What didn’t screen well on the announcement was that Elevidys’ fourth-quarter sales were quite dampened by a pretty severe flu season? Six patient infusions got pushed back into early 2026, which now makes it clear to a lot of investors how sensitive the revenue base can be to short-term clinical logistics. If we see this kind of headwind again, I expect the market to react in a similar way.

Now, you may think six patients getting their therapy pushed back doesn’t seem like a lot, but keep in mind Sarepta’s offerings are extremely high value. It's a single-dose gene therapy, and unlike typical chronic medications, where you get thousands of patients contributing small recurring revenue, Elevidys generates tens of millions of dollars per patient per infusion. If we take a quick estimate of the figures, the deferral of six patient infusions likely reduced the treatment’s 4Q25 revenue by $15 to 18 million if we assume each infusion generates about $2.5 to $3 million. So, against the sales figure of $110.4 million, this would be a 13% to 16% impact.

The good news is that the revenue is only delayed, and it doesn’t actually imply there is a lack in the actual demand for that quarter. If the revenue from those six deferred patients were recognised, sales would be more in line with what we saw in 3Q25. However, the bigger picture for the year has now shown a gradual decline in Elevidys sales.

An Update On SRP-1005

Just prior to the preliminary earnings, Sarepta announced the submission of a clinical trial application for SRP-1005. It’s set to be a pretty important strategic expansion of their pipeline beyond just muscle diseases and into neurodegeneration, specifically Huntington’s disease. Now, this is an area with profound unmet need and no approved disease-modifying therapies, so even though it’s set to be a long journey for Sarepta, they're showing steps in the right direction for long-term expansion. They have a planned INSIGHTT Phase 1 study, which is expected to begin sometime in 2Q26.

Now, the study will actually represent the first-in-human evaluation of this subcutaneously administered siRNA. In short, it’s designed to cross the blood-brain barrier and then selectively reduce huntingtin protein in deep brain regions, which are most affected by the disease. Sarepta is also leveraging its transferrin receptor-based delivery platform for the candidate. The aim here would be to achieve consistent CNS exposure while avoiding the dosing limitations that we usually see with intrathecal or higher systemic approaches. If they can achieve this, I think it will be a key differentiator if validated clinically and gain some investor interest. We should also keep in mind that they are still in early stages, so there is inherently a much higher-risk in this program. However, it's a good sign that management clearly has intent here to build a much longer-term growth engine beyond Duchenne.

An Attractive Valuation

One of the more attractive viewpoints on Sarepta stock would be the valuation. After the sell-off post preliminary results, the valuation appears even more compellingly discounted relative to their own revenue scale and the sector peers. It is even more appealing for me, given we’re starting to see a somewhat established commercial base. They hold a market cap of $2.4 billion and an enterprise value of $2.8 billion, against $1.86 billion in expected FY25 net product revenue. So the stock trades at just 1.2x EV to sales on a trailing twelve-month basis and 0.9x price to sales. It implies a 65% to 75% discount to the Health Care sector median, and an even steeper discount when you compare it to Sarepta’s own five-year averages.

Now, I think these revenue-based multiples are especially appropriate for Sarepta since we're, so far, only seeing inconsistent profitability but quite meaningful, diversified product sales across Elevidys and the PMO franchise. Traditional earnings metrics, of course, remain non-meaningful since they have that elevated R&D and commercialisation spend, and forward price-to-earnings estimates for this year and 2027 sit in the 10x to 11x range, which suggests the market is pricing in limited durability or growth. If we take this together, the sharp compression in sales and book-value multiples, even though they hold almost $1 billion in cash and a multi-year runway, tells us that the stock is valuing Sarepta more like a distressed biotech right now, rather than a scaled rare-disease commercial company. For me, that points to a lot of potential upside if execution stabilizes and revenue durability is sustained.

Looking Ahead

Next for Sarepta, we’ll get the final 4Q25 and FY25 earnings sometime in late February. That should give us a clearer picture of profitability, revenue breakdowns, and more in-depth guidance for 2026, but the fact that they had to reiterate that $500 sales floor in the preliminary results makes me err on the side of caution. The preliminary results are actually quite disappointing for me and a lot softer than I had expected. It wasn’t the outcome I had hoped to see, so I think it's best for investors to hold and wait until we get a bigger picture on earnings about demand.

data.title: Has Nintendo Entered The Era Of Incremental Innovations?
data.description:
When Switch 1 debuted in March 2017, the Japanese console maker (TSE:7974) was trading at JPY2,317 a share. The console and game developer has risen steadily since reaching the high of JPY14,500 in August 2025.

Switch 1, of course, wasn’t the only Nintendo console released between then and now. Multiple variations such as Switch Lite and upgrades such as Switch OLED followed, decent launches that supported the share performance.

When Switch 2 was revealed in April 2025, the market reacted immediately valuing the company up by JPY4,000 a share. Shortly after the official launch in June, however, the price moderated back to pre-release levels.

Although Switch 2 sales already broke the record set by Switch 1, with the forecast set at 19 million units by end-FY2026, the stock nonetheless fell 17% from June to the close of 2025. During the same period, Japan’s Nikkei 225 rose 13%, so Nintendo was definitely not weighed down by market weakness.

The big question, therefore, is whether this setback is a buying opportunity for investors — or a sign of the new normal for Nintendo. My take is that the stock will continue to underperform due to three fundamental shifts within Nintendo.

Shift 1: From low-cost profit leader to yet another loss-making console maker

The genius of Nintendo has always been about how they packaged cheaper hardware components into a form factor that appealed to the general audience. This was the main idea of my previous article where I compared the gaming company to Apple given the use of clever hardware and software integrations to make their consoles perform better regardless of specs.

Nintendo's ability to offer its hardware at lower price points relative to peers had to do largely with the operating model, its making money on both consoles and games, unlike other competitors running a razor blade model (i.e., lose money on consoles and make money selling games).

That, in turn, has been about having a focused software side of the business. Selling first party titles produced in-house allowed Nintendo to get the best of both worlds. As a result, Nintendo got away with offering fewer discounts for their games than most developers competing across multiple platforms.

Console

Nintendo Switch 2

Hybrid

449-499 USD

Source: Various by author

Nintendo positioned itself as the more affordable option at different specs and thus a more palatable purchase for casual gamers. And yet it was profitable, practically the only manufacturer to be turning a profit from console sales; in FY2017, for one, Switch 1 contributed to a 261% jump in net profit.

This enviable economic moat — the ability to charge less and get into the hands of more consumers (4 of top 10 consoles ever sold came from Nintendo) while still earning a profit — may be starting to erode. Switch 2 is reported to have launched at loss-making prices.

Profit implications should become apparent soon enough. Operating income surged after the launch of Switch 1. While it is still somewhat early for Switch 2, a similar surge seems unlikely — at best, stagnation.

Shift 2: From defining an era of handheld gaming to playing it safe

By not joining the race to ever-higher hardware specs, Nintendo had to innovate harder to make its consoles attractive to gamers. Although there have been some misses, overall it is fair to say that Nintendo has made culture defining consoles, breaking away from the industry's red ocean. This helped justify the exceptional market reaction ahead of Switch 2: in June 2025, it was trading at a PE of 56.8x — well above Microsoft's 36.3x and Sony's 21.7x.

The script, however, may be changing. With Switch 2, Nintendo appears to have played it safe rather than trying to redefine the rules the way it used to.

By the time Switch 2 launched, the handheld gaming space already looked crowded, with devices like Steam Deck and ROG Ally having made major inroads. Add mobile phones to the mix, and consumer expectations have become about access to AAA titles and massive game libraries. Judging by tech specs and titles alone, Nintendo’s appeal is starting to fade. Even first-party titles, the company’s biggest advantage, might not be enough this time.

Device

~20

Low / last‑gen

4000 ++

Source: Various by author

Shift 3: First-party titles, Nintendo's area of dominance becoming harder to create

Game count and console power were never really an issue for Nintendo. In fact, Nintendo’s control of software in terms of first party titles was one of its biggest draws. With graphics optimized for hardware and franchises with massive fan bases, Nintendo was able to create a magnetism around its consoles and software that rivals found hard to imitate.

Software is such a big part of Nintendo's business that it can be reasonably seen as the main driver of its success. Over the past two decades, Nintendo has sold more than 4 billion units of software, both first and third party titles, more over the company's lifetime. On Switch alone, 62 first party games have been created, with top ten titles accounting for 23% of total sales.

Console

4,216

Source: Nintendo by author

However, emerging trends indicate that Nintendo is going to find it harder to emulate their software success of the past.

To begin with, party titles are getting harder to build, with longer timelines and increased costs. This is an industry wide problem, but to make up for fewer games developed in a single year and the increased complexity of production, Nintendo has been increasing prices for their games — which, of course, does not sit well with the consumers. A limited game library is also the reason why Nintendo has been re-releasing games on the Switch.

Although Switch 2 is excelling in terms of unit sales, the software side of things is not moving at the same rate so far. The four months to end-September since the launch of Switch 2 saw 20 million devices sold. In contrast, in the first six months of FY2017, a total of 7 million Switch 1 consoles were sold with software sales of 27 million units. That means 2 games purchased for each Switch 2 versus 4 titles per Switch 1.

Overall, there seems to be a massive slowdown across the software business across all Nintendo consoles. This means less reason for people to buy Nintendo consoles when compared to top competitors with bigger game libraries. The steadily declining number of titles published by Nintendo reflects this shift.

Valuation

Since my original analysis a little over three years ago, NTDOY has gone up by 48% — pretty decent relative to S&P 500's 58%. Valuation though has risen far more sharply, with the 12-month trailing P/E at one point in mid-2025 passing 60x. It has since compressed to 30x, so the stock is currently more or less as expensive as Microsoft (MSFT) and, of course, far more expensive than stable Asian peers NetEase (NTES) and Sony (SONY).

Catalysts

This negative thesis would be challenged if the bulls' enthusiasm around the current Switch 2 cycle (but also higher-margin software and digital revenue) actually turns out to hold water. And the only way it would show up is through a sustained improvement in growth, particularly in operating income — which has declined by almost 20% in the last three years. ​

Having more game releases for Switch 2 and them catching on Mario-style would be another nice-to-have.

Conclusion

Nintendo just feels different these days, and that could explain why the market has softened on the stock recently. This is not entirely the company's fault; after all, the industry is being reshaped by rapid technological change. Still, while near-term March 2026 results may still come in line, sustained outperformance could be a thing of the past.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Restaurant Brands International: Market Remains Cautious, So Buying Opportunities Also Remain
data.description:
It has been nearly three months since my previous coverage of Restaurant Brands International, Inc. (QSR), or simply RBI. The stock price has stayed flat, but the uptrend still holds, which reflects cautious optimism among investors. Macro and market challenges are still present, but I believe that its strategic operations and robust fundamentals can shield it. Valuation remains very attractive and suggest some upside potential. Technicals adhere to it as bullish signals persist despite the recent price dip.

Restaurant Brands International: How It Has Been Recently

Household brands like Burger King, Tim Horton's, and Popeye's are not complete safe from the impact of inflation, heavy competition, and predatory pricing. Yet, Restaurant Brands International, Inc's strategic management and business portfolio diversification helped stabilized growth while managing cost pressures amid its expansion. These things were visible in its most recent performance.

During Q3 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $2.45B, up by 6.5% YoY from $2.29B. This exceeded my estimated revenue growth of 5.0% for the quarter. All revenue components rose, suggesting its stronger performance of both company-operated and franchised restaurants. Tim Horton's remained the primary component as it comprised 46% of the total revenue. Meanwhile, Firehouse Subs had the highest revenue growth of 12%.

In addition, revenue growth in Q3 2025 was higher than in my previous coverage. One factor to consider is its solid restaurant management. Another is its continued store openings. I will discuss these two aspects more later. Lastly, it maintained its strong pricing power despite stubborn inflation during the quarter.

More interestingly, QSR remained highly efficient. The increase in the operating costs and expenses was proportionate to revenue growth. Once again, we can attribute this to QSR's strategic restaurant management. As a result, its revenue growth was more than enough to cover costs and expenses. This helped QSR keep a high operating margin of 27.1% versus 25.2%, respectively.

Challenges

As a business in the restaurant industry, QSR or RBI is highly susceptible to macroeconomic risks. Inflation has already weakened from its 14-month high of 3.0% to 2.7%, but that doesn't automatically invite consumption. Positive inflation means that prices are still increasing, which can affect purchasing power and discretionary spending. Tariffs may worsen inflation since they raise the costs incurred by domestic producers, which may be passed on to domestic producers.

I also think that RBI faces heavy competition since its products are quite homogeneous to its peers. For instance, Burger King competes with McDonald's (MCD), Five Guys, Jack In The Box (JACK), and Wendy's (WEN).

It also competes with fast-casual restaurants like Shake Shack (SHAK) due to stubborn inflation. Amid rising cost pressures, I believe that BK and fast-food peers may hardly capitalize on low pricing. If the trend persists, their menu prices may keep increasing, which will narrow the gap between fast-food and fast-casual burgers.

Why RBI Is Still Solid

Despite these headwinds, I believe that RBI is still well-positioned.

1. RBI Maintains Strategic Restaurant Management.

RBI knows how to optimize its operations and balance growth with profitability by managing its store openings and closings in line with the market dynamics. For instance, it reduced its Burger King restaurants from 7,119 to 7,043 to manage restaurant operating margin amid heavy competition and cost pressures. Despite this, its pricing power and demand remained strong. This was supported by its improved marketing strategy, restaurant modernization through its Reclaim the Flame plan, and menu innovation. Iconic brands and items like its flame-broiled Whopper helped maintain its popularity. I also believe that its Whopper Detour and Order From McDonald's campaigns a few years ago still have an impact on it until today. After all, its provocative humor combined with its technologies remains effective. So, the average sales per restaurant last Q3 2025 reached $419,708 versus $406,096 YoY.

Burger King Average Sales Systemwide Sales Restaurant Count Average Sales Per Restaurant
Q3 2024 $2.891B 7,119 $406,096
Q3 2025 $2.956B 7,043 $419,708

In other restaurants, it takes advantage of their stronger demand by opening more stores. It continues to increase its restaurant count in other brands like Tim Horton's. Thankfully, the openings remain fruitful. Its systemwide sales rose by 4.8%, which was stronger than in the previous year at 3.8%. In addition, each restaurant generated more sales at $447,700 versus $433,400.

Tim Horton's Average Sales Systemwide Sales Restaurant Count Average Sales Per Restaurant
Q3 2024 $1.952B 4,504 $433,400
Q3 2025 $2.029B 4,532 $447,700

Popeye's also maintained an upward trajectory. This can be sustained by its increasing Latin American footprint.

2. RBI's Business Model Remains Flexible. RBI continues to balance the distribution of company-operated and franchised restaurants in its business portfolio. It maintains a significant number of company-owned restaurants. But it also has a lot of franchised restaurants to increase its revenue stream. I'll say this move is strategic because having franchisees mean having a secure revenue stream at very manageable costs/expenses. It also maintains synergy between the two segment because RBI also supplies food and packaging to its franchisees, especially Tim Horton's. This allows it to generate supply chain revenues aside from franchise fees. I also like that it owns or rents properties or real estate for many of its franchisees. That is why the line item in the income statement is not just franchise revenues but franchise and property revenues. With that, it also earns rental and/or sublease income.

Lastly, the presence of Firehouse Subs does not confine RBI in the fast-food segment. Firehouse Subs can be considered a fast-casual restaurant since it sells sandwiches filled with premium deli meats and/or tuna salad topped with tomatoes, lettuce, and onions using various bread types. These are premium ingredients, so FHS appear to be capitalizing on value propositions rather than on cheaper pricing. This makes the restaurant more flexible to inflation.

3. Interest Rate Cuts May Spur Its Performance. In just a quarter, the Fed has already reduced the interest rate by 75 bps. This can increase borrower and investor confidence and stimulate economic activity. As a result, employment and wages may also improve, which can increase purchasing power. In addition, this can lower borrowing costs and make credit cards more accessible and attractive. Meanwhile, Credit cards can become cheaper and support discretionary spending. Lastly, interest rate cuts can help RBI manage its profitability and liquidity since it can lower the cost of variable-rate borrowings.

4. Robust Fundamentals Ensure Longevity. Because RBI is still profitable, it also enjoys positive cash inflows. The positive operating cash flow is still enough to cover capex. The difference of $996 is its FCF, which means that it converted 14.3% of its sales or revenues in three quarters using the FCF/Sales Ratio. This is also enough to cover dividends and debt repayments. So, it's not surprising that its cash and cash equivalents remain above $1B.

Moreover, its borrowings have a decent maturity profile because 99% of the total will not mature within a year. To be more specific, 40% has a maturity of four years and longer. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 4.66x, which means that RBI can repay the whole amount at once even if it doesn't have to. Hence, it has an adequate capital buffer to cover its operating capacity and dividends.

Projection/Valuation

QSR's revenue growth of 6.5% last Q3 2025 exceeded my 5% expectation in my previous coverage. But I think that the market is cautious ahead of the Q4 2025 release due to the price dip recently.

With respect to its 2025 performance, I believe that my projection in the previous coverage remains logical. As a recap, I set its revenue growth at 10% to derive $9.28B. In my view, this is reasonable since revenue growth in Q1 and Q2 2025 reached 21.4% and 15.7%, respectively. If we average the revenue growth for three quarters, we will derive 14.4%. So, I think my assumption is still conservative. Moreover, the TTM revenue is $9.26, which is close to my estimated amount. For COGS, I set it as a percentage of revenue. However, I increased it by 2.0% to price in more risks. From $5.942 before, my estimated COGS is now $6.122B. Meanwhile, I left the remaining items unchanged. Nonetheless, my adjustment affected the operating profit, NOPAT, and EBITDA.

For its valuation, I believe that QSR remains a buy. To derive a TP or target price using a forward-looking approach, I used the Dividend Discount Model. I based its annualized dividend of $2.48 on the most recent payout of $1.08 per quarter. Meanwhile, I got the dividend growth rate of 3.99% using the 2021-2025 CAGR. Lastly, I estimated the cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. First, I set its risk premium at 5.0%. Next, I estimated Beta using the SLOPE function on the weekly price change on MS Excel. I got 0.62, which is exactly the same as the one on Yahoo Finance. Using this, the TP is $74.92. If I add a 5% allowance, it will decrease to $71.18. Either way, QSR is still trading at a reasonable level.

Technicals

QSR is still bullish even after the recent dip. It still trends upwards with higher highs and lows. Also, it moves above the 50-day and 200-day SMA, which means that the uptrend still holds. After all, price momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. In addition, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, so market sentiments are still optimistic.

On the other hand, its MACD and RSI show rebound attempts. The MACD line is now above the signal line, which shows that buying volume is starting to pick up again. The histogram is back to positive territory. This implies that buying volume has outpaced selling volume again. Lastly, RSI is increasing consistently with the price, so we cannot assume or detect any bearish divergence yet. There are also buying opportunities since it is still below the overbought region.

Conclusion

Restaurant Brands International, Inc. is still solid for me due to its robust growth and stable margins. Fundamentals, flexible business model, and prudent restaurant management are the things I believe can shield it against macroeconomic headwinds. Its valuation is also justifiable and suggests upside potential. Technicals adhere to my view as bullish signals persist despite the recent dip. Hence, I am reiterating my buy rating.

data.title: Fast-Growing Brazilian Bank AGI Pursues U.S. IPO For Further Expansion Plans
data.description:
AGI Is Growing And Profitable

AGI Inc. (AGBK) has filed to raise growth capital in an IPO of its Class A common shares, according to SEC F-1 registration information.

AGBK provides a range of banking and financial services to underserved individuals in Brazil.

The company is growing its balance sheet portfolio of loans quickly and expanding into asset management services.

I’ll provide an update when we get more information about the IPO’s pricing and valuation.

What Does AGI Do?

Campinas, Brazil-based AGI Inc was founded to develop banking services for underserved individuals in Brazil who have not been reached by digital-only banks.

Management is led by founder and Chairman Mr. Marciano Testa, who has been with the firm since its inception in 2018 and was previously co-founder of Instituto Caldeira and holds a degree in economics.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Bank accounts

  • Debit and credit cards

  • Payroll loans

  • Social Security deposits and loans

  • Life insurance

  • Unsecured consumer credit

  • Other financial services.

AGI makes money primarily through interest charged on its various loan products, but also through ancillary fees and interest income from fixed-income securities it owns.

The pie chart below shows the percentage contribution of its various revenue sources in 2024:

AGBK has booked fair market value investment of $500 million through September 30, 2025, from investors, including the founder and others.

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have dropped as revenues have grown sharply, as the figures below indicate:

Selling, G&A

Expenses vs. Revenue

Period

Percentage

Nine Months Ended Sept. 30, 2025

15.3%

Year Ended December 31, 2024

18.4%

Year Ended December 31, 2023

18.6%

(Source - SEC.)

The Selling, G&A efficiency multiple, i.e., how many dollars of additional revenue are produced from each dollar of Selling, G&A expense, rose to 1.9x in the most recent reporting period, as shown in the table below:

Selling, G&A

Efficiency Rate

Period

Multiple

Nine Months Ended Sept. 30, 2025

Year Ended December 31, 2024

(Source - SEC.)

What Is AGI’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Grand View Research, the Brazilian retail banking industry was an estimated $58.7 billion in 2024 and is forecasted to exceed $107 billion by 2033.

This represents a projected CAGR of 6.9% from 2025 to 2033, a moderate rate of growth.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are the expected growth of public sector banks at the fastest rate of growth versus private sector banks.

Also, the chart below shows the historical and expected future growth path of the Brazil retail banking industry through 2033:

The company is also active in the digital banking industry in Brazil, which is expected to grow at a much higher rate of growth in the years ahead.

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • Banco Mercantil

  • Nubank

  • Banco Inter

  • C6 Bank

  • Stoneco

  • Mercado Pago

  • Large incumbent banks

  • Fintechs

  • Mid-size, regional financial institutions

  • Others.

The firm operates over 1,100 "Smart Hubs" - asset-light retail locations - that it uses to connect with non-digital native people in Brazil, including social security beneficiaries, low-income workers and retirees.

It also uses digital platforms for younger or more technology-oriented prospective clients.

AGI is an accredited payor for Brazil's Social Security Institute, distributing pensions and retirement benefits and providing the company with an entry point with users for payroll-linked loans.

So, AGI is operating in a changing landscape within Brazil, one in which the digital banks have reduced the legacy banks’ dominance and the growth of Pix Automatico (automated payments) capabilities have lowered switching costs and barriers, favoring AGI.

Below is a thumbnail SWOT analysis for AGI:

Strengths

  • Hybrid retail/digital engagement model, Social Security Institute [SSI] accreditation brings new customer inflows, AI-enhanced automation, core business focused on secured lending.

Weaknesses

  • Dependence on the SSI for prospect inflow, lack of history in asset management, reliance on external platforms for deposit funding, complexity managing large-scale Smart Hub network.

Opportunities

  • Positive demographic trends in Brazil, easier onboarding in private payroll market, seeking domestic and international acquisitions, AI tool integrations.

Threats

  • Intense competition from incumbents, regulatory uncertainties, macroeconomic volatility in Brazil.

AGI’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Sharply higher top line revenue.

  • Falling net interest margin.

  • Reduced net charge-off percentage.

  • Increasing net income and net margin.

Below are the major financial results from the firm’s current registration statement:

$ 80,959,000

Cash Flow From (Used in) Operations

Period

Nine Months Ended Sept. 30, 2025

$ (97,111,850)

Year Ended December 31, 2024

Year Ended December 31, 2023

$ (6,330,230)

(Glossary Of Terms.)

(Source - SEC.)

AGI’s IPO Plan

AGBK intends to sell its Class A common shares in an IPO of what may reach $200 million, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares of the IPO as detailed in the first prospectus filed.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

Class B shares will have ten (10) votes per share and will be owned by the founder and Chairman, who will have voting control of the company immediately post-IPO.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the normal & customary 180-day period following the effective date of the IPO prospectus.

According to AGI’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

We intend to use the net proceeds from this offering for general corporate purposes. Additionally, we may use a portion of the net proceeds to acquire or invest in businesses, products, services, or technologies. However, we do not have agreements or commitments for any material acquisitions or investments at this time.

(Source - SEC.)

The firm’s presentation for its roadshow isn’t available yet.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, the company is subject to a number of judicial and administrative proceedings with an estimated accrued liability of approximately $74 million and management said that

"We may be materially and adversely affected by unfavorable outcomes in pending legal proceedings or our inability to post judicial collateral or provide guarantees in pending legal or administrative proceedings."

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Citigroup, Bradesco BBI, BTG Pactual, Itaú BBA, Santander, Société Générale, XP Investments and Oppenheimer & Co.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of September 30, 2025, AGI had $57 million in cash and equivalents and $127 million in borrowings and bank debt.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2025, of negative ($57.7 million).

AGI has been growing its loan portfolio rapidly, allocating capital toward this goal.

Its negative cash flow results are consistent with this recent history, hence the need for the IPO to generate capital for further growth.

Larger, more established banks in Brazil has reached self-sustaining positive cash flow.

So, AGI’s stage of development is more indicative of a "high-growth scaler expanding its balance sheet," rather than a large scale bank.

As a result, its performance relies on a growing deposit base to fund operational cash outflows.

AGI Is Growing Profitably

AGI is seeking U.S. public capital market funding for its general, unspecified growth plans.

The company’s financials have shown higher topline revenue, dropping net interest margin, but lower net charge-off percentage and higher net income and net margin.

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenues have increased and its Selling, G&A efficiency multiple increased to 1.9x in the most recent partial year reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and to retain all available funds for its expansion initiatives and balance sheet lending capacity.

AGI’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent moderately on capital expenditures in the trailing twelve-month period.

The firm’s growth strategy includes gaining larger share of wallet from existing client base, launching new Smart Hubs and expanding throughout Brazil and beyond.

The market opportunity for providing banking and lending services to underserved individuals in Brazil is large and expected to grow at a reasonably solid rate of the growth in the coming years.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its ‘foreign private issuer’ and ‘emerging growth company’ status, which allows management to produce less information for shareholders.

Also, the company does business primarily in Brazil, and the Brazilian Reais has generally tended to depreciate against the U.S. dollar over extended periods of time.

Since it acquires a variety of software and other operating expenses denominated in U.S. dollars, a depreciation of the Reais would result in growing operating expenses.

The company also has country and regulatory concentration risks and customer demographic concentration risks.

AGI was suspended and then reinstated in the middle of 2025 by the INSS (Social Security Institute - Brazilian National Social Security administration agency) for various payment processing issues.

It was also briefly suspended in late 2025 and reinstated on January 12, 2026 by the INSS for payroll loan deduction “alleged irregularities and allegedly harmful practices against INSS beneficiaries” until the company settled to provide the INSS with increased documentation and future oversight.

If the company is suspended again, or for a longer period, this could have a severely negative impact on its ability to generate payroll-secured loans, a major revenue source for the firm.

When we learn more details about the IPO’s pricing and valuation assumptions, I’ll provide a final opinion.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Are The Stars Aligning For A New Bull Run In Homebuilder Stocks?
data.description:
The relatively weak performance of shares in the homebuilder industry for the past two calendar years is, on first glance, surprising. A long-running under-investment in home building has created a housing shortage at a time of a demographic-driven rise in demand. But holding an ETF that tracks shares in the homebuilding industry has only matched the return of the broad equity market over the past five years but at a cost of sharply higher volatility.

It’s timely to wonder if the fortunes of homebuilder stocks are poised to improve. One catalyst for change is the slide in borrowing costs. The average long-term US mortgage rate, based on the 30-year fixed rate, fell to 6.16% last week, the lowest in over three years, according to mortgage buyer Freddie Mac.

Softer borrowing costs should be a net plus at a time when, according to a recent Goldman Sachs estimate: “At least 3-4 million additional homes beyond normal construction need to be built to address the shortage in US housing supply and boost affordability.”

Despite the supply-demand tailwind, several factors have slowed the industry, including a decline in housing affordability that’s accelerated since the pandemic and various land-use restrictions, Goldman analysts note. Last week’s news that US housing starts in October fell to lowest level in nearly six years reminds that a quick fix doesn’t look imminent.

But the ongoing supply shortage, in time, will likely create conditions for a stronger run in homebuilder stocks. Politics may be a supporting factor too. The White House in recent weeks has reportedly met with industrial officials on ways to improve affordability and how the administration could help.

In a possible early hint of things to come, market sentiment has turned firmly bullish on homebuilders this year. The S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB) has rallied more than 12% year to date through Thursday’s close (Jan. 15), far above the broad stock market’s 1.5% gain, based on the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

Encouraging, but a couple of weeks could be noise. Meanwhile, homebuilders are still far behind equities overall for the past several years. For the trailing one-year window, for instance, SPY is handily outperforming XHB: 18.2% vs. 6.4%.

If the tide begins turning in favor of homebuilders, the investment case will look more convincing in relative terms against stocks generally. One way to monitor the trend is by tracking the ratio of XHB to SPY.

Despite the recent pop in homebuilder shares, it’s not clear that the industry’s relatively weak performance since 2024 has run its course. A stronger sign that the cycle is turning would arise when the 50-day average for this ratio recovers and rises above the 200-day average. On that basis, the jury’s still out if homebuilders are poised to outshine the broad market in 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
